coredata.dc:title: Employing novel N-doped graphene quantum dots to improve chloride binding of cement
coredata.dc:creator: He, Haijie
E, Shuang
Wen, Tiande
Yao, Jun
Wang, Xiaogang
He, Chuang
Yu, Yang
coredata.dc:description: One of the most primary reasons for durability deterioration of reinforced concrete (RC) structures is reinforcement corrosion caused by chloride ion invasion. However, there is still a lack of efficient additives to enhance the anti-chloride-corrosion behavior of RC structures. Herein, highly dispersed and cost-efficient N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) are firstly applied to significantly increase the chloride binding performance of cement. Specifically, N-GQDs with high dispersity and low cost are successfully synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method. And the typical equilibrium test determines that after 14-day exposure to 3 M NaCl solution, the incorporation of 0.2 wt% (by weight of cement) N-GQDs makes the chloride binding ability of cement paste sharply boost by 134%. Moreover, in light of phase compositions analyses from XRD, TGA and SEM, the chloride binding mechanism of cement pastes modified by N-GQDs is rationally ascribed to the fact that the addition of N-GQDs enormously increases the physically adsorbed chloride ions and slightly promotes chemically bound chloride ions. This work firstly provides a novel carbon-based nanomaterial to efficiently enhance the chloride binding of cement, expected to improve the durability of RC structures.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chloride binding
Graphene quantum dots
Cement paste
Phase compositions
Binding mechanism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analyses of phase compositions from different specimens
Chloride binding behaviors of various cement pastes
Phase compositions of various cement pastes
Chloride binding mechanism of N-GQDs-modified cement pastes

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China-Youth Science Fund

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Sciences for Youth Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China-Youth Science Fund (No. 52208273 ) and the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province ( LQ21E080003 ). Furthermore, the authors thank Xinfang Cui from Shiyanjia Lab ( www.shiyanjia.com ) for UV-vis and PL spectra measurement.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The morphology and structure of the as-obtained N-GQDs: (a) TEM image (with a size distribution and HRTEM images, inset); (b) AFM image and (c) the height profile along the white line in the AFM image; (d) Raman spectra.
Surface groups, chemical compositions and optical properties of N-GQDs: (a) FTIR spectra;(b) full range XPS spectra; (c) high-resolution XPS C1s, (d) O1s and (e) N1s spectra; (f) PL spectra.

(a) UV–vis spectra and (b) maximum PL spectra of N-GQDs aqueous solution with different concentrations before and after 3-day standing; (c) UV–vis spectra and (d) maximum PL spectra of N-GQDs simulated concrete pore solution with various contents before and after 3-day standing.
(a) The Cb values of diverse groups at different immersion times in 3 M NaCl solution; (b) The increased rates of Cb values from N-GQDs-adding groups at diverse immersion times.
XRD patterns of different cement pastes (a) before and (b) after 14-day exposure to NaCl solution (CH: Ca(OH)2; Cc: CaCO3).
Phase compositions of diverse cement pastes after 14-day immersion in NaCl solution calculated by Rietveld method based on XRD patterns (wt.%).
(a) TGA/DTG of various cement pastes after 14-day immersion in NaCl solution; (b) Chloride distribution in Fs and C-S-H gels. (Ettr: ettringite; Ms: monosulfate).
Typical SEM images of various groups after 14-day exposure to 3 M NaCl solution: (a) N0, (b) N2, (c) N3 and (d) N4.
Schematic illustration of chloride binding mechanism of cement pastes with and without N-GQDs.

row.entry: Mass ratio of N-GQDs (by weight of cement)

head.ce:title: Employing novel N-doped graphene quantum dots to improve chloride binding of cement

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Civil Engineering and Architecture

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Civil and Transportation Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, Guangdong, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work and should be considered co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: One of the most primary reasons for durability deterioration of reinforced concrete (RC) structures is reinforcement corrosion caused by chloride ion invasion. However, there is still a lack of efficient additives to enhance the anti-chloride-corrosion behavior of RC structures. Herein, highly dispersed and cost-efficient N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) are firstly applied to significantly increase the chloride binding performance of cement. Specifically, N-GQDs with high dispersity and low cost are successfully synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method. And the typical equilibrium test determines that after 14-day exposure to 3 M NaCl solution, the incorporation of 0.2 wt% (by weight of cement) N-GQDs makes the chloride binding ability of cement paste sharply boost by 134%. Moreover, in light of phase compositions analyses from XRD, TGA and SEM, the chloride binding mechanism of cement pastes modified by N-GQDs is rationally ascribed to the fact that the addition of N-GQDs enormously increases the physically adsorbed chloride ions and slightly promotes chemically bound chloride ions. This work firstly provides a novel carbon-based nanomaterial to efficiently enhance the chloride binding of cement, expected to improve the durability of RC structures.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The durability of reinforced concrete (RC) structures is a hotspot and difficulty of civil engineering [1]. Durability deterioration of RC structures not only causes serious hidden safety hazards, but leads to huge economic losses. For example, only in China, the economic loss related to corrosion damage of RC structures was more than 1 trillion CNY, accounting for approximately 1.4% of GDP in 2014 [2]. In the complex coastal environment and deicing salts-applied locations, chloride invasion dominates the main deterioration mechanism. Chloride ions reach the surface of reinforced bars through pores or cracks of concrete, and destroy the surface passivation layer after excessing the threshold value. This brings about reinforcement corrosion, and then the rust expansion of reinforced bars accelerates the crack formation in concrete, subsequently resulting in more cracks in concrete and even spalling of concrete cover [3,4]. Eventually, the concrete durability is tremendously impaired.

To address the issue above, multitudes of technologies are explored to improve the resistance to chloride corrosion of concrete, including corrosion inhibitor [5], anti-corrosion coating [6], cathodic protection [7] and chloride binding [8]. Of which, corrosion inhibitor, anti-corrosion coating and cathodic protection have been extensively demonstrated the favorable inhibition of chloride-induced reinforcement corrosion, but they suffer from an unavoidable drawback. Namely, they cannot fundamentally eliminate free chloride ions in concrete. As is well known, the intruded chloride ions in concrete fail to be consumed, and function as a catalyst to repeatedly damage the passivation layer of reinforcement [9,10]. Hence, more effective and propitious anticorrosion of RC structures can be realized by removing free chloride ions in concrete. Differently, the chloride binding technique is able to transform the free chloride ions into bound chloride ions. More specifically, through promoting the formation of calcium silicate hydrate (C–S–H) gels or Friedel’s salt (Fs)/Kuzel’s salt (Ks), this technique enables concrete to enhance the chloride physical adsorption or chemical binding, thereby reducing chloride ion concentration in concrete and improving the durability of RC structures [11].

Nanomaterials have become a focus of the chloride binding technique due to their high specific surface area, high reactivity and low dosage. Currently, diverse nanomaterials have been used to improve the binding capacity of concrete, involving nano Al2O3, layered double hydroxides (LDHs), carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and graphene oxide (GO). For instance, in 2019, Tan’s group revealed the application potential of nano Al2O3 in chloride immobilization. In detail, the adding of nano Al2O3 could heighten the immobilized chloride capacity of cement pastes even at the long age. When replacing cement with 4 wt% nano Al2O3, the immobilized chloride ability of cement pastes was enhanced by 29% after 180-day curing compared with that of reference sample at the age of 28 days. And the related immobilized chloride mechanism was assigned to the promoted chloride chemical binding via more formation of Fs and Ks [12]. The similar result was found by Jiang’s group [13,14]. Specifically, the incorporation of 5.0 wt% nano Al2O3 boosted the bound chloride content by 37.2% at 0.05 mol/L NaCl solution [13]. However, owing to the pozzolanic reaction of nano Al2O3, its addition decreased the Ca/Si ratio of C-S-H gels, thus reducing the chloride physical adsorption [12]. Besides nano Al2O3, LDHs have also been employed to advance the chloride binding performance of cement-based materials. Yang et al. revealed that regardless of 7- or 28-day curing, the addition of 1.5 wt% CaFeAl-NO3 LDHs in the simulated sea sand mortar sample reduced the free chloride by more than 32% than those of the blank sample, and assigned the excellent chloride binding performance of LDHs-modified cement to the formation of LDHs-Cl, Fs, and Ks [15]. Due to their structural memory effect, calcined LDHs (CLDHs) could display larger improvement on the chloride binding of cement composites [16]. Jin’s group claimed that after the addition of 7.5 wt% CLDHs, the chloride binding behavior of mortar was remarkably augmented by 65% [17]. More recently, carbon-based nanomaterials have been verified to enhance the bound chloride content of cement matrix. Chenzhi Li found that CNTs could facilitate the formation of more C-S-H gels, thereby increasing chloride physical adsorption of concrete [18]. Inspired by this, Khayat’s group proved the enhancing chloride binding capacity of GO-incorporated cement paste, and reported that adding 0.2 wt% GO rendered the chloride binding of cement pastes boost by 46% [19]. To sum up, considerable progress has been made in existing research, there are still some obvious shortcomings are inevitable: (1) Poor dispersion. All the nanomaterials mentioned above share a poor dispersion, and inescapably aggregate in cement matrix. This not only enormously compromises their prospective effectiveness, exacerbating rheological behavior, etc., but also sharply raise the economic cost on account of their compensatory incorporation to the cement composites; (2) High cost. The above nanomaterials, notably CNTs and GO, are expensive, immensely hindering their actual application; (3) Adverse effect on fluidity. The polycarboxylate superplasticizer must be used to adjust the fluidity of nanomaterial-modified cement-based materials. This not only further increases costs, but also has a negative impact on the chloride binding of cement paste [20]. Therefore, developing a highly dispersed and cost-effective nanomaterial to promote the chloride binding of cement matrix is urgently necessary.

Fortunately, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as the newest member of carbon-based nanomaterials, not only inherit some properties from CNTs and GO, but also possess the merits of excellent dispersion stability, facile preparation, cost-effectiveness, low toxicity and favorable environmental friendliness [21–23]. These characteristics endow GQDs potential application prospects in the chloride binding technique for cement matrix. However, to the best of our knowledge, there are no reports to investigate chloride binding properties of GQDs-adding cement-based materials until now. If GQDs are verified to promote the chloride binding performance of cement composites, it will offer a novel highly dispersed and low-cost nanomaterial to improve the durability of RC structures. Therefore, it highly requires a pioneering study to explore the feasibility and effectiveness of GQDs used in the chloride binding technique.

In this work, given the fact that N-containing groups are able to further improve the hydrophilicity and provide more reactive sites for cement hydrates [24], novel N-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) with high dispersity and low cost are employed to promote the chloride binding of cement pastes for the first time. Specifically, N-GQDs are successfully fabricated by a facile one-step hydrothermal method using nitronaphthalene and ammonia as the carbon and nitrogen sources respectively. They display the outstanding long-term dispersion stability in simulated concrete pore solution (namely, Ca(OH)2 saturation solution), demonstrated by ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) and photoluminescence (PL) spectra. The equilibrium tests are performed to determine chloride binding properties of cement pastes including 0, 0.025, 0.05, 0.1 or 0.2 wt% N-GQDs, revealing the feasibility and effectiveness of N-GQDs on chloride binding. And the related mechanism is rationally proposed according to phase compositions analyses of X-ray diffraction (XRD), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and scanning electron microscope (SEM). This study firstly demonstrates the fascinating prospects of GQDs in the chloride binding technique, expected to improve the anti-chloride-corrosion performance of RC structures.

The chemicals including naphthalene, concentrated nitric acid (HNO3), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), ammonia, and calcium hydroxide (Ca(OH)2) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All the chemicals were analytically pure and used without further treatment. The Ca(OH)2 saturation solution served as simulated concrete pore solution. Deionized (DI) water was used throughout the experiment. The Portland cement (PC) was PI 52.5R based on the Chinese standards of GB175 and GB176. Table 1 and Table 2 presented the physical properties and chemical composition of PC.

N-GQDs were prepared by a facile one-step hydrothermal method using nitronaphthalene and ammonia as the carbon and nitrogen sources respectively. In detail, nitronaphthalene were obtained as follows: 3.2 g naphthalene was added to 80 mL concentrated HNO3 and subsequently stirred at 80 °C for 12 h; Then after centrifugation, washing with DI water and drying, nitronaphthalene powder was prepared. Subsequently, 1 g nitronaphthalene, 0.4 g NaOH and 73 mL DI water were treated by ultrasonication for 15 min. The resulting mixture was combined with 0.4 mol ammonia (27 mL), and then transferred to a 150 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and heated 8 h at 200 °C. After cooling to room temperature, the reaction mixture was neutralized and subjected to centrifugation. The supernatant was filtered through 0.2 um membrane and dialyzed to remove insoluble carbon product, large particles, sodium salt and unreacted molecules. Finally, the solution was concentrated through rotary evaporation, and then freeze-dried to obtain N-GQDs powder.

To investigate the effectiveness of N-GQDs on the chloride binding performance of cement paste, five specimens with different dosages (0, 0.025, 0.05, 0.1 and 0.2 wt%; by weight of cement) were prepared and labeled as N0, N1, N2, N3 and N4, respectively. To ensure consistency, the water-cement ratio (w/c) was fixed at 0.5 for all specimens. The specific mix proportions were listed in Table 3 . In the progress of preparing cement pastes, N-GQDs were dissolved in the mixing water, and then the N-GQDs aqueous solution was added into cement under the stirring of the mixer. The prepared mixture was poured into 15 mm × 15 mm × 15 mm silicone mold and covered with plastic film to prevent moisture evaporation. After 1-day curing, the specimens were demolded and transferred to a curing chamber with 20 ± 2 °C and over 95% relative humidity for 28-day curing.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Talos F200S, USA) and atomic force microscopy (AFM, Brucker MFP-3D infinity, Germany) were carried out to determine the morphology of the as-prepared N-GQDs. And the structure information of N-GQDs was recorded by XRD (Brucker D8 Advance, Germany) and Raman spectra (Thermo Scientific DXR, USA). Moreover, FTIR (Thermo Scientific Nicolet-6700, USA) spectra ranging from 600 to 3600 cm−1 and X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS, Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250, USA) were performed to reveal the surface chemical states and elemental compositions of N-GQDs. In addition, Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis, Perkin Elmer Lambda 750, USA) and photoluminescence (PL, Hitachi F7000, Japan) spectra were adopted to record optical properties of N-GQDs.

The specimens after 28-day curing were placed in an oven at 60 °C until their quantities were constant. Subsequently, these dried samples were ground into powder with a size less than 75 μm in a ball mill for 15 min at 350 r/min. Then, 4 g of cement powder from each sample was placed in a 100 mL plastic tube containing 40 mL 3 M NaCl solution. To avoid chloride salt precipitation owing to water evaporation, all tubes must be sealed and shielded from sunshine and extreme temperatures. At the same time, these plastic tubes were violently shaken for 3 min at 24-hour intervals by hand to ensure the full contact of chloride and cement powder. The obtained mixture was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min to separate the liquid supernatant after different immersion times (1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 12 and 14 d). The liquid supernatant was utilized to assess the concentration of free chloride ions, and the precipitate was employed for the phase analyses. Using an automated potentiometric titrator, the concentration of free chloride ions was measured with 0.1 mol/L silver nitrate (AgNO3) [25]. Eq. (1) was used to calculate the binding chloride content (Cb ) of each sample:
(1)
C b
=
35.453 ×
V 0
×

)

was the bound chloride content (mg/g paste);
V 0
presented the volume of the solution (mL) of the mixture;
C 0
and
C f
represented the initial and final chloride concentrations of the NaCl solution; W belonged to the mass of the specimen powder (g) added to the NaCl solution.

Eq. (2) was utilized to calculate the quality of Fs crystals, postulating that the mass loss of Fs crystal measured via TGA included 6 molecules of main layer water in the structure Fs crystals [26]. Additionally, Eq. (3) was adopted to confirm the chloride binding content from Fs crystals (
C
b

).

Where

= 561 g/mol (the molar mass of Fs with the chemical composition of Ca4Al2Cl2(OH)12·4H2O or C3A . CaCl2·10H2O);

was the mass loss related to 6 molecules of main layer water in the structure of Fs crystals (wt.%);

=18 g/mol (the molar mass of water) and
M Cl
= 35.5 g/mol (the molar mass of Cl element). It must be mentioned that Fs with a Cl/Al ratio of 1 was a simplification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analyses of phase compositions from different specimens
ce:section.ce:para: To better understand the underlying influence of N-GQDs on chloride binding properties of cement pastes, XRD, TGA and SEM were conducted to analyze the phase changes of the specimens before and after exposure to 3 M NaCl solution. The specific sample preparation processes were as follows: the as-obtained centrifuged sediment above in Section 2.5 was put into the oven at 60 °C until its weight was constant, and then passed a 0.75 um sieve before phase characterization. XRD patterns were characterized by a D8 Advance high-resolution X-ray diffractometer (Brucker, Germany) using a Cu Kα radiation source at 40 kV voltage and 40 mA current with a 6°/min scanning speed. To quantificationally analyze XRD patterns based on the Rietveld method, 20 wt% ZnO was added to each sample, and then mixed thoroughly in an agate mortar. TGA and differential thermogravimetric (DTG) curves were performed ranging from 30 to 1000 °C in a flowing N2 atmosphere with a heating speed of 10 °C /min adopting a STA-409PC thermogravimetric analyzer (Netzsch, Germany). SEM images were taken on a MIRA LMS scanning electron microscope (TESCAN, Czech) equipped with Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectrometer (EDS).

Based on the excellent performance of N-containing groups and the fact that GQDs can inherit some properties from CNTs and GO, N-GQDs are prepared by a facile hydrothermal approach. To prove the successful formation of N-GQDs, the morphology, structure and surface groups of the as-prepared product are characterized in detail. As for morphology of the product, its particle size distribution, lattice structure and topographical height are measured by TEM and AFM (Fig. 1 a and 1b). The TEM image (Fig. 1a) shows the uniform dispersion of the product without any aggregation. By randomly measuring more than 150 particles, the product presents a size distribution of 0.4–5.6 nm and an average size of ca. 2.96 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image illustrates that the product owns favorable crystalline structures with well-resolved lattice fringes, and its lattice spacing of 0.21 nm is consistent with the graphene (100) plane. AFM image discloses that the topographical height of the product falls in the range of 0.4–0.7 nm, indicating the product is composed of 1–2 layers of graphene. According to the definition of GQDs (i. e., GQDs normally are defined as small carbon nanoparticles with less than 10 nm in size [27,28]. Meanwhile, they possess single/several layers of graphene crystalline structures in the carbon cores, occasioning their height smaller than the lateral dimension), the as-synthesized product is GQDs. Furthermore, as displayed in Raman spectra (Fig. 1d), there are two distinct peaks at 1345 and 1580 cm−1, attributed to the D band (disordered sp3 defects) and G band (sp2 carbon) respectively, and the intensity ratio of the two bands (I D/I G) is 0.82. This finding implies that the as-prepared GQDs have a high degree of crystallinity, well corresponding to the result of TEM. In brief, these observations from morphological and structural characterization convincingly evidence that the as-obtained product is high-crystallinity GQDs with a small average size of around 2.96 nm.

Surface groups and chemical compositions of the GQDs are confirmed by FTIR and XPS spectra (Fig. 2 a-e). As illustrated in Fig. 2a, the stretching and in-plane bending vibrations of N–H at 3056 and 1289 cm−1 respectively, as well as the stretching vibration of C–N at 1421 cm−1 [29,30], confirm successful introduction of N into GQDs. Furthermore, the characteristic peaks at 3243, 1762 and 1034 cm−1 demonstrate the presence of –OH, C=O and C–O. These O-containing groups are also beneficial to improve the dispersity of GQDs [27], and advance the cement hydration [31]. Besides, the stretching vibration of C=C bonds at 1554 cm−1 manifests that GQDs hold the sp2 C structure [32], in accordance with the results from TEM and Raman spectra. XPS measurement further testifies the findings of FTIR spectra. In the full range XPS spectra (Fig. 2b), there are three obvious peaks from O1s (532 eV), N1s (400 eV) and C1s (284 eV) as well as one weak peak from impurity Na+ (496 eV). The contents of C, O and N elements are 59.51%, 29.47% and 10.35% respectively, further suggesting the presence of N in GQDs. Thus, the as-obtained GQDs are N-GQDs. Additionally, in the high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 2c), three peaks are ascribed to C=C/C–C (284.8 eV), C–O/C–N (286.3 eV) and C=O (288.7 eV) [33,34]. Clearly, the content of C=C/C–C bonds is dominant, accounting for 72.11%. This further illustrates that N-GQDs contain the abundant sp2 C structure. The high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) can be deconvolved into two peaks: C–O (532.7 eV) and C=O (531.2 eV). This indicates the surface of N-GQDs contains rich oxygen-containing groups, in good accordance with the result of FTIR spectra. And the spectrum of N1s (Fig. 2e) indicates two peaks at 399.8 and 401.7 eV for –NH2 and C–N respectively [35], further implying that N-GQDs have a large number of N-containing groups. In a summary, the analyses of surface groups and chemical compositions strongly demonstrates that the as-synthesized GQDs include abundant N- and O-containing groups as well as highly crystalline sp2 C structures. Hence, the N-GQDs are successfully prepared.

The optical performance is the intrinsic characteristic of GQDs [27]. UV–vis and PL spectra are used to determine the optical properties of the as-synthesized GQDs. With respect to the UV–vis spectra of N-GQDs aqueous solution (Fig. 3 a), there are two absorption peaks: a strong peak at 296 nm and a weak peak at 358 nm, belonging to the π–π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains and O/N-containing groups respectively [34,36]. Regarding PL behaviors of N-GQDs, their aqueous solution shows yellow color excited by the sunlight and emits the bright green fluorescence exposed to a 365 nm UV light (Fig. 3a). As depicted in PL spectra (Fig. 2f), the N-GQDs present an excitation-independent PL action, and have the excitation/emission maximum at 470/507 nm. The unique optical performance of N-GQDs provides a new way to assess their dispersion stability in aqueous solution and simulated concrete pore solution.

To sum up, the morphological, structural and surface groups characterization solidly indicates that N-GQDs are successfully prepared by a facile hydrothermal method, and they possess the features of a small average size of approximately 2.96 nm, high-crystallinity sp2 C structures, abundant N- and O-containing groups and unique optical performance. These characteristics may contribute to N-GQDs having prospective uses in the chloride binding approach.

It is commonly recognized that whether or not nanoparticles can fully play a positive function depends on how well they disperse in cementitious materials [37]. To qualitatively validate the dispersion stability of N-GQDs, UV–vis spectra and PL spectra (Fig. 3) before and after 3-day standing are recorded on a basic of their unique optical properties. Visibly, no matter whether in aqueous solution (Fig. 3a and 3c) or simulated concrete pore solution (Fig. 3b and 3d), the peak intensity of N-GQDs presents a progressive increase with augmenting the content of N-GQDs in both UV–vis spectra and maximum PL spectra. This indicates that N-GQDs possess a distinguished dispersity regardless of in aqueous solution or simulated concrete pore solution, owing to their abundant surface groups particularly the nitrogen-containing groups. More importantly, the intensities of UV–vis absorption peaks and maximum PL emission peaks from the N-GQDs aqueous solution are essentially consistent before and after 3-day standing (Fig. 3a and 3b), regardless of N-GQD content. And the same observation occurs in N-GQDs simulated concrete pore solution with various concentrations (Fig. 3c and 3d). This strongly demonstrates the long-term dispersion stability of N-GQDs in aqueous solution and simulated concrete pore solution. As a result, the aforementioned optical tests qualitatively evidence that in aqueous solution and simulated concrete pore solution, N-GQDs are able to overcome one of the most troublesome disadvantages of nanomaterials used in cement-based materials, namely, agglomeration. And they exhibit the eminent long-term dispersion stability, laying a solid foundation for the potential applications of N-GQDs in cement matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chloride binding behaviors of various cement pastes
ce:section.ce:para: To clarify the influence of N-GQDs on the chloride binding property of cement pastes, the chloride binding content (Cb ) of each cement paste is measured adopting the standard equilibrium test following immersion in 3 M NaCl solution for varying periods. The results (Fig. 4 a) suggest that Cb values from all the cement pastes markedly increase over immersion time. At the same time, with the addition of N-GQDs, the chloride binding performance of cement pastes is evidently improved. It is notoriously believed that the agglomeration of nanomaterials in cement composites inescapably compromises their beneficial effects. While regarding N-GQDs, within the dosage of 0.2 wt%, the more N-GQDs added, the greater enhancement in Cb value. This verifies the outstanding dispersion of N-GQDs in cement pastes. Obviously, N4 group with the incorporation of 0.2 wt% N-GQDs owns the highest Cb value of 57.87 mg/g after 14-day immersion, significantly boosting by 134% with reference to blank group (i. e., N0 group). Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 4b, after immersing 3 d, all the N-GQDs-modified groups display the highest increased rates of Cb values regardless of N-GQDs addition. And the increased rate of the Cb value from N4 group is highest, reaching 270%. This unveils that N-GQDs cause cement pastes to immobilize free chloride ions more rapidly. When the immersion time ranges from 7 to 14 days, the increased rates of Cb values from cement pastes containing N-GQDs share an identical tendency and similar values. It should be pointed out that in the prior study, the incorporation of 0.2 wt% GO raised the Cb value of cement paste by only 46% after 14-day immersion in 3 M NaCl solution under similar experimental procedure [19]. It is much smaller than the increased rate of Cb value (134%) from the cement paste modified by 0.2 wt% N-GQDs. This is strong evidence that N-GQDs have a more effective promotion on the chloride binding performance of cement pastes compared with GO. In consequence, the as-obtained N-GQDs, serving as a novel and promising carbon-based nanomaterial, can remarkably enhance the chloride binding abilities of cement pastes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phase compositions of various cement pastes
ce:section.ce:para: To reveal the chloride binding mechanism of cement pastes modified by N-GQDs, XRD patterns, TGA/DTG curves and SEM images are employed to comprehensively investigate the phase compositions of cement pastes containing different contents of N-GQDs before and after 14-day exposure to 3 M NaCl solution. As depicted in XRD patterns (Fig. 5 a), all the cement pastes before immersion present identical peaks, such as the hydration product of Ca(OH)2 (CH, peaks at 18.1°, 28.7° and 34°) and the carbonization product of CaCO3 (Cc, peaks at 29.4°). This suggests that the introduction of N-GQDs fail to generate new hydration products. Besides, it has been widely accepted that the intensity of the strongest characteristic peak at 18.1° arising from CH is directly proportional to its quantity [38,39]. Thus, the higher the peak at 18.1°, the more the hydration product of CH. Distinctly, the strongest characteristic peak of CH at 18.1° is visibly improved with the addition of N-GQDs. And N4 group with the maximum amount of N-GQDs possess the strongest CH characteristic peak. This means that the introduction of N-GQDs can markedly promote the cement hydration probably owing to the seeding effect of N-GQDs. And due to the outstanding dispersity of N-GQDs, the more the adding of N-GQDs, the higher the hydration degree of the cement. More importantly, the content of the primary hydration product of C–S–H gels is in direct proportion to the amount of CH formation. It can be inferred that more C–S–H gels is generated in N-GQDs-modified cement pastes. While C–S–H gels are principally responsible for chloride physical adsorption through mutual attraction between charges. As a result, physical adsorption for free chloride ions of cement pastes is enhanced by incorporating N-GQDs. And N4 group with more N-GQDs can achieve better physical adsorption effect on free chloride ions. As displayed in Fig. 5b, after 14-day immersion, new diffraction peaks at 11°, 22° and 23° corresponding to Fs are found in all the cement pastes, whether or not N-GQDs are introduced. It represents that Fs-induced chloride chemical binding is accomplished. It should be noted that Fs and Ks are formed by the reaction between monosulfate phases and free chloride ions. And when the concentration of free chloride ions is low, the reaction product tends to be Ks; Oppositely, Fs predominates the reaction product at the high content of free chloride ions (1 mol/L) [40]. In present work, on account of the higher content of free chloride ions much more than 1 mol/L, Fs crystals as the reaction product are responsible for the chloride chemical binding.

Since the intensity of the strongest characteristic peak of Fs at 11° is low, comparing the difference between Fs peaks of different groups is challenging. To overcome it, Rietveld method is used to quantitatively analyze the Fs content and other phase compositions of diverse groups based on the XRD patterns. As illustrated in Fig. 6 , after 14-day exposure to NaCl solution, the mass ratio of unhydrated products (C2S and C3S) in cement pastes gradually decreases with the increase of N-GQDs addition. On the contrary, the content of C–S–H gels in cement pastes reveals an increasing trend. These findings further reflect that N-GQDs can promote the cement hydration. And more introduction of N-GQDs has a more visible accelerating effect on cement hydration. In terms of Fs, its composition gradually increases from 8.7% of N0 group to 10.1% of N4 group, implying that the incorporation of N-GQDs can slightly promote Fs-caused chemical binding for free chloride ions of cement pastes. Consequently, on basic of the observation of XRD, N-GQDs are capable of visibly enhancing chloride physical adsorption, and slightly increasing chloride chemical binding of cement pastes, and eventually promote the Cb value. And within the dosage of 0.2 wt%, more introduction of N-GQDs can endow cement pastes with the better chloride binding performance.

Apart from XRD patterns, TGA/DTG is also employed to get more detailed information about the exact contents of phases in different group. And the observation of TGA/DTG well supports the result drawn from XRD patterns. As indicated in TGA curves (Fig. 7 a), all the cement pastes show similar curves with a downward trend after 14-day immersion in NaCl solution. But more mass losses appear in N-GQDs-containing groups than blank group, signifying more generation of hydration products in cement pastes modified by N-GQDs. DTG curves of all the groups are exhibited in Fig. 7a, distinguishing various phases based on mass losses at different temperature intervals. There are identical mass loss humps at the range of 90–140 °C, 140–190 °C, 280–360 °C, 450–500 °C and 600–850 °C in all the cement pastes, attributed to decomposition of C–S–H gels/Ettringite, monosulfate, Fs, CH and Cc respectively [26,41,42]. This further substantiates that the introduction of N-GQDs cannot change the types of hydration products, well coinciding with the finding of XRD. More specifically, C–S–H gels as the main hydration product lose bound water ranging from 40 to 600 °C with a central peak at around 100 °C; While the crystalline water of ettringite is inclined to be removed at 105–114 °C. Since C–S–H gels possesses a much larger mass ratio than ettringite, the mass loss hump ranging 90 from 140 °C primarily belongs to C–S–H gels. It is well known that the greater the magnitude of the mass loss peak, the more content the corresponding phase. Clearly, the mass loss peak of C–S–H gels derived from N-GQDs-modified cement pastes is much stronger than that of blank group, and the more addition of N-GQDs contributes to the more generation of C–S–H gels. This phenomenon firmly demonstrates that the incorporation of N-GQDs conduces to promoting the cement hydration to form more C–S–H gels, thereby significantly improving the chloride physical adsorption. Within the dosage of 0.2 wt% N-GQDs, the enhancement effect is proportionally increased. As a result, N-GQDs can remarkably promote the chloride physical adsorption performance of cement pastes by more formation of C–S–H gels. With respect to monosulfate (the weight loss peak from 140 to 190 °C) and Fs (the weight loss peak in the range of 280–360 °C), their weight loss peaks are marginally stronger than blank group. This reflects that although the incorporation of N-GQDs can generate more monosulfate and Fs to boost the chemical binding for free chloride ions, the magnitude of the increase is not significant, which is in accordance with the deduction of XRD. In brief, the qualitative result of TGA/DTG shows that the addition of N-GQDs can evidently advance the chloride physical adsorption but slightly improve the chloride chemical binding of cement pastes.

It has been widely recognized that the chloride binding of cement-based materials can be divided into two categories: chloride physical adsorption and chemical binding. The former is controlled by C–S–H gels, while the latter is dominated by Fs at high concentration of chloride ions. Other than C–S–H gels and Fs, other phases have a negligible effect on the chloride binding capacity [19]. To confirm the bound chloride distribution in C–S–H gels and Fs after 14-day immersion in 3 M NaCl solution, the chemically bound chloride (
C
b
Fs
) induced by Fs is calculated using Eq. (3). And then the physically adsorbed chloride (
C
b
CSH
) caused by C–S–H gels is ascertained by Eq. (4):
C
b
CSH
=
C b
-
C
b
Fs
. The calculation result (Fig. 7b) indicates that compared with N0 group, N-GQDs-incorporated groups tremendously advance
C
b
CSH
values, but marginally improve
C
b
Fs
values. The higher the introduction of N-GQDs, the larger
C
b
CSH
and
C
b
Fs
values. Additionally, with the increasing content of N-GQDs, the proportion of the
C
b
CSH
value to Cb value augments. In N4 group, the proportion is up to 71%, much larger than the value (roughly 30%) of GO-modified cement pastes.

SEM images (Fig. 8 ) are employed to directly observe the morphologies of various phases in cement pastes after 14-day exposure to 3 M NaCl solution. As illustrated in Fig. 8a, some similar hexagonal flaky crystals with a size distribution of 1–3 μm belong to Fs in blank group, and several larger-sized hexagon sheets with the size more than 3 μm are assigned to CH crystal. Meanwhile, a number of amorphous gel-like C–S–H agglomeration owns a haphazard distribution among other hydration products. Regarding N2 group with 0.05 wt% N-GQDs, more C–S–H gels covering on Fs and CH crystals can be found, while Fs crystal has no obvious increase (Fig. 8b). This corroborates that N-GQDs can advance the formation of C–S–H gels, thereby distinctly improving the chloride physical adsorption. With respect to N3 and N4 groups (Fig. 8c and 8d), after adding more N-GQDs, the quantity of C–S–H gels possesses an evidently incremental increase, while the number of Fs crystals unobtrusively augments. Even due to the cover of C–S–H gels, it is difficult to find them. Thus, the findings of SEM well agree with the results of XRD and TGA/DTG. N-GQDs show a significant impact on the chloride physical adsorption of cement pastes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chloride binding mechanism of N-GQDs-modified cement pastes
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the comprehensive analyses above, a reasonable chloride binding mechanism of cement pastes modified by N-GQDs is proposed (Fig. 9 ). Namely, the incorporation of N-GQDs obviously promotes the formation of C-S-H gels and slightly increases the content of Fs, thus greatly promotes the physically adsorbed chloride ions and slightly promotes chemically bound chloride ions. More specifically, as shown in Fig. 9a, in blank group, owing to incomplete hydration, the quantity of C-S-H gels is insufficient. At the same time, monosulfate phases are also not adequate, giving rise to the shortage of Fs crystals. Thus, both the values of chloride physical adsorption and chemical binding are small. Regarding N-GQDs, they can inherit some properties such as nucleation effect from CNTs and GO, thereby advancing the crystallization of hydrates and improving the hydration process. Meanwhile, as GO and CNTs, N-GQDs are also able to tightly bind to C–S–H gels through the bridge effect [18,42]. By virtue of the synergetic effect of nucleation and bridge, more C–S–H gels with high density and high stiffness can be formed. More importantly, N-GQDs with a ultrasmall average size of ca. 2.96 nm and high dispersity further enhance the synergetic effect above. Therefore, after the incorporation of N-GQDs, more formed high-density and high-stiffness C–S–H gels are capable of remarkably boosting physically adsorbed chloride ions (Fig. 9b). And the abundant N-/O-containing groups on the surface of N-GQDs not only conduce to further accelerating the cement hydration, but also greatly improve the dispersibility in cement paste, thus endowing N-GQDs with higher enhancement effect on the chloride physical adsorption. Eventually, when the dosage is within 0.2 wt%, the higher the N-GQDs addition, the stronger the chloride physical adsorption of cement pastes. Besides C–S–H gels, N-GQDs also increase the hydration of aluminum-containing minerals, and possibly advance the transformation of ettringite to monosulfate phase (Fig. 9b), which is proved by XRD and TGA/DTG. However, the promotion of aluminum-containing minerals hydration and the transformation of ettringite to monosulfate phase is slight. Consequently, the chloride chemical binding of cement pastes is marginally increased by the addition of N-GQDs. In a word, the chloride binding mechanism of cement pastes modified by N-GQDs is rationally ascribed to the fact that the addition of N-GQDs enormously increases the physically adsorbed chloride ions and slightly promotes chemically bound chloride ions.

In summary, to enhance the anti-chloride-corrosion behavior of RC structures, N-GQDs with high dispersity and low cost are firstly applied as a novel nano-additive to promote the chloride binding performance of cement paste. And in light of phase compositions analyses of XRD, TGA/DTG and SEM, the chloride binding mechanism of N-GQDs-incorporated cement pastes is proposed. The main conclusions are as follows:

(1) Low-cost N-GQDs are successfully prepared by a facile hydrothermal method. The as-prepared N-GQDs have a small average size of roughly 2.96 nm, and share high-crystallinity sp2 C structures, abundant N-/O-containing groups and unique optical abilities. More importantly, in aqueous solution and simulated concrete pore solution, N-GQDs present an eminent long-term dispersion stability, allowing them to satisfy the requirements for potential applications in cement matrix.

(2) The typical equilibrium test is employed to determine the chloride binding properties of various cement paste specimens with different addition of N-GQDs (0, 0.025, 0.05, 0.1 and 0.2 wt%). The results demonstrate that with the raising of N-GQDs dosage, the chloride binding content of cement pastes is gradually increased. When the incorporation of N-GQDs is 0.2 wt%, the chloride binding content value is 57.87 mg/g after 14-day immersion in 3 M NaCl solution, significantly boosting by 134% compared with blank group.

(3) According to the analyses of XRD, DTG/TGA and SEM on phase compositions of different cement pastes, the chloride binding mechanism of cement pastes modified by N-GQDs is rationally ascribed to the fact that the addition of N-GQDs greatly promotes the formation of C–S–H gels and marginally boost the generation of Fs crystals. It leads to enormous advancement of the chloride physical adsorption and slight improvement of the chloride chemical binding.

These findings demonstrate the promising potential of N-GQDs in enhancing the anti-chloride-corrosion performance of cement pastes, expecting to provide a new high-dispersion and cost-efficient nanomaterial to promote the durability of RC structures in the complex coastal environment and deicing salts-applied locations.

Haijie He: Data curation. Shuang E: Writing – review & editing. Tiande Wen: Software. Jun Yao: Resources. Xiaogang Wang: Investigation. Chuang He: Writing – review & editing. Yang Yu: Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China-Youth Science Fund (No. 52208273) and the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (LQ21E080003). Furthermore, the authors thank Xinfang Cui from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for UV-vis and PL spectra measurement.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of natural carbonation on chloride binding behaviours in OPC paste investigated by a thermodynamic model
J. Build. Eng.

Cem. Concr. Res.
